ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Singaporean economic rice with mixed meat toppings; 'cooked rice dish' (FOODON_00002572) used as the closest defensible ancestor term.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for Singaporean cai fan / economic rice. A000E (composite rice dish) is used as the closest defensible category anchor.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code identified for this specific Singaporean mixed rice dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard plate of economic rice with three meat dishes (one fried) typically weighs approximately 400 g, comprising steamed white rice and three portions of mixed meat dishes served at a hawker centre.

nutrient_source.title: Economic Rice (3 meats, 1 fried) — HPB Singapore Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for Economic Rice (3 meats, 1 fried), cr_id F05100043. All per-100g values are sourced from this record with confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — white rice (steamed, Asian-style) as primary GI anchor; composite dish GL estimated from serving carbohydrate content
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS et al., Diabetes Care 2021
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 72 is sourced from the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed white rice (Asian-style), which is the dominant carbohydrate component of this dish. The composite dish GI is treated as equivalent to the rice component given that the meat and fat components do not materially alter the GI of the rice base. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (72) × carbohydrate per serving (71g for 400g plate at 17.75g/100g) / 100 = 51.2. Input record cited GI 72 with confidence 0.591 from an embedded GI database; this is consistent with the University of Sydney reference value and has been upgraded to the approved source.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 290 mg/100g with low dietary fibre (0.86 g/100g), no vegetable component, and saturated fat from fried meat contributing to arterial stiffness
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size, replace the fried meat component with a steamed or braised option, and add a vegetable dish to improve potassium and fibre intake to offset the sodium load of approximately 1,160 mg per standard 400 g plate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High-GI white rice (GI 72) with 17.75 g/100g carbohydrate and only 0.86 g/100g dietary fibre produces a very high glycaemic load of approximately 51 per serving, worsened by fried meat increasing fat-driven insulin resistance
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should reduce the rice portion by at least half, substitute the fried meat with a lean braised or steamed protein, and add a non-starchy vegetable dish to lower the overall glycaemic load and improve postprandial glucose control.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Fried meat component contributes saturated fat and likely trans fat from repeated frying oil use, combined with 290 mg/100g sodium and low fibre, creating an atherogenic dietary pattern
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with cardiovascular disease should avoid the fried meat component entirely, choose braised or steamed protein alternatives, reduce sodium exposure by limiting sauce additions, and add a vegetable dish to improve fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-fried meat introduces advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) and the absence of vegetables means no anti-inflammatory nutrients (omega-3, antioxidants, polyphenols) are present
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should replace the fried meat with steamed fish or braised chicken and add at least one vegetable dish to introduce antioxidant and anti-inflammatory benefit.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Economic Rice (3 Meats, 1 Fried) — known in Singaporean Chinese as 经济饭 (jīng jì fàn) and in Malay as Nasi Ekonomi — is a ubiquitous Singaporean hawker dish consisting of a base of steamed white jasmine rice topped with a self-selected combination of cooked dishes. In this variant, the diner selects three meat-based dishes, one of which is deep-fried (commonly fried chicken, fried fish, or fried tofu with meat). The other two dishes are typically braised, stir-fried, or steamed preparations such as braised pork belly, steamed egg, or stir-fried minced meat. The dish is served on a single plate or tray and is a staple working-lunch option at hawker centres and coffee shops across Singapore. Nutritionally, per 100 g the dish provides 206 kcal, 11.29 g protein, 9.84 g fat, and 17.75 g carbohydrate, with 290 mg sodium and only 0.86 g dietary fibre. The high-GI white rice base (GI 72) combined with the fried meat component and low fibre content creates a high glycaemic load per serving (approximately 51 GL units for a standard 400 g plate). The dish is clinically relevant for individuals managing hypertension, Type 2 Diabetes, and cardiovascular disease due to its sodium content, high glycaemic load, and saturated fat from the fried component. Texture is varied: the rice is soft and slightly sticky, braised meats are tender and savoury, and the fried component is crispy with a rich umami flavour from seasoning and frying oil.
common_user_queries: How many calories in cai fan 3 meat 1 fried?
Is cai fan suitable for hypertension?
Economic rice with fried chicken calories

confidence_reason: Nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05100043) with confidence 0.95. This is an exact match from the primary approved regional source for Singaporean dishes. GI value of 72 is consistent with the University of Sydney GI Database for steamed white rice (Asian-style), the dominant carbohydrate component.
notes: Saturated fat estimated at 2.8 g/100g based on HPB Singapore Foodo typical values for mixed meat rice dishes with one fried component; the HPB source record does not separately report saturated fat for this composite entry. The following per_100g fields are set to -1 because detailed fatty acid profile, micronutrient breakdown, and moisture data for this composite hawker dish are not separately reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for F05100043 as of 2026, and no supplementary approved source (USDA FoodData Central, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, FAO/INFOODS SEA) provides a sufficiently close composite match to supply these values: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, potassium_mg, cholesterol_mg, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g. Halal status is not confirmed as the dish composition varies by hawker stall and may include pork-based items. The glycaemic load per serving of 51.2 is calculated for a standard 400 g plate (17.75 g carbohydrate per 100g × 4 × GI 72 / 100). The input record listed GI source as 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved source; this has been replaced with the University of Sydney GI Database reference for steamed white rice (GI 72), which is consistent with the input value.
